name: Pulau Tekong
body: Every cohort of conscripts inherits the island's stories before they ever set foot on it, handed down in the days before enlistment by brothers and cousins who swear each word is true. The most enduring is the tale of a company of recruits, Charlie Company in nearly every telling, that vanished on a night exercise and was quietly struck from the records, so that the bunk you sleep in may be one no roll call admits exists. The detail that the men were erased, not merely lost, is what gives the story its grip: it is not a tragedy so much as a cover-up, and you are sleeping inside it.

Then come the smaller, surer tales, the ones told in low voices after lights-out: the extra pair of boots marching in the dark, the section that counts one too many heads, the figure standing at the foot of the bed at three in the morning, the soldier who hears his own name called from the treeline in a voice he knows is not human. They are domestic horrors, scaled to barracks life, and they spread because every recruit already lies awake exhausted in an unfamiliar bunk, primed to mistake a shadow for a watcher and a draft for a breath.

What lingers is how the island folds these stories into the ordinary machinery of training, so that fear becomes part of the routine: the route march that passes the overgrown plot everyone avoids, the sergeant who will not explain why a certain block stays empty, the way the older recruits go quiet near a particular bend in the perimeter track. Long after the men leave, the island keeps them by keeping the stories, and a new intake arrives each cycle to be told, on their first uneasy night, exactly how many of them there are supposed to be.
note: This is a rare living tradition, passed mouth to mouth through national service and mutating with each intake rather than surviving in any written source. It is worth collecting first-hand precisely because the same core motifs (the vanished company, the extra head at roll call, the named voice from the trees) surface independently from men who never met, which is its own kind of unsettling.

Treat the "erased company" as folklore, not documented event: there is no credible record of a Charlie Company being deleted from the rolls, and responsible collecting means resisting the urge to manufacture dates, unit numbers, or casualty figures to make it sound official. The honest value here is ethnographic, charting how the legend changes between cohorts, which details harden and which drop away, and why an island of tired young men reliably grows the same ghosts. Be respectful of the real institution and the real people who serve there; the interest is in the storytelling, not in passing rumor off as history.
